ORDER AND STIPULATION FOR MOTION TO BE HEARD BY A MEMBER OF THE CALIFORNIA BAR SERVING AS A TEMPORARY JUDGE

LAW AND MOTION, 302, RULING: ARGUED AND THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING: DEFENDANTS TIMOTHY MARTEN AND MARTEN CLINIC OF PLASTIC AND RECONSTRUCTIVE SURGERY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS DENIED. DEFENDANT MARTEN ("DEFENDANT") HAS NOT MET HIS INITIAL BURDEN OF PROOF TO SHOW THAT THERE ARE NO TRIABLE ISSUES OF FACT. (AGUILAR V. ATLANTIC RICHFIELD CO. (2001) 25 CAL.4TH 826, 850.) FIRST, DEFENDANT HAS NOT PRESENTED SUFFICIENT EVIDENCE TO SHOW THAT THE STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS IS NOT SUBJECT TO EQUITABLE ESTOPPEL. DEFENDANT HAS NOT PRESENTED ANY EVIDENCE TO CONTROVERT THE ALLEGATIONS IN THE COMPLAINT ON THIS ISSUE. LIKEWISE, THE SEPARATE STATEMENT DOES NOT ADDRESS THIS ISSUE. SECOND, PLAINTIFF'S EVIDENTIARY OBJECTIONS TO THE DECLARATION OF DR. TOTH AND THE DECLARATION OF NAFIA WESTON ARE SUSTAINED. THE SUPPLEMENTAL DECLARATION OF NAFIA WESTON, FILED IN REPLY, DOES NOT CURE THE EVIDENTIARY OBJECTION AS TO THE MEDICAL RECORDS; IT STILL FAILS TO PROVIDE A SUFFICIENT BASIS TO AUTHENTICATE THESE RECORDS AND TO ESTABLISH THAT THEY ARE BUSINESS RECORDS. SEE GARIBAY V. HEMMAT, 161 CAL. APP. 4TH 735 (2008). EVEN IF THE COURT WERE TO CONSIDER THE TESTIMONY OF DR. TOTH, IT DOES NOT ESTABLISH THAT PLAINTIFF CANNOT MAKE A PRIMA FACIE CLAIM FOR MEDICAL NEGLIGENCE, BATTERY AND NEGLIGENT MISREPRESENTATION. IT DOES NOT ADDRESS THE ALLEGATIONS THAT PLAINTIFF DID NOT CONSENT TO DEFENDANT CHANGING THE SHAPE OF HER MOUTH, USING ANY PROCEDURE THAT PENETRATED HER FACE OR NARROWED HER FACE. AT THE VERY LEAST, THIS CREATES A TRIABLE ISSUE OF MATERIAL FACT. (SEE DECLARATION OF PLAINTIFF FILED IN OPPOSITION, 7-8.) (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER.) JUDGE: MARLA J. DISCOVERY 302, DEFENDANT TIMOTHY MARTEN'S MOTION TO COMPEL PLAINTIFF, JANE DOE, TO PROVIDE FURTHER ANSWERS TO DEPOSITION QUESTIONS AND PRODUCE DOCUMENTS; REQUEST FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS: DEFENDANTS MOVE FOR FURTHER RESPONSES TO 11 DEPOSITION QUESTIONS, FOR PRODUCTION OF A DOCUMENT REFERENCED IN THE DEPOSITION, AND FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS. PLAINTIFF'S DEPOSITION HAS TAKEN PLACE OVER 5 DAYS SO FAR. THE RECORD CONTAINS LONG COLLOQUY BETWEEN COUNSEL, NUMEROUS SPEAKING OBJECTIONS AND INSTRUCTIONS NOT TO ANSWER ON A VARIETY OF GROUNDS. DEFENDANTS WAITED UNTIL THE LAST DAY TO FILE THIS MOTION WITHOUT ENGAGING IN MEET AND CONFER OTHER THAN WHAT HAD OCCURRED ON THE RECORD AT THE DEPOSITION. PLAINTIFF CONTENDS THE MOTION IS UNTIMELY AND THAT DEFENDANTS FAILED ADEQUATELY TO MEET AND CONFER. THE COURT FINDS THAT THE MOTION IS TIMELY AS IT WAS FILED WITHIN 60 DAYS OF DEFENDANT RECEIVING THE TRANSCRIPT. THE COURT ALSO FINDS THAT ALTHOUGH DEFENDANTS SHOULD HAVE ENGAGED IN FURTHER MEET AND CONFER, THE PARTIES DID MEET AND CONFER ON THE ISSUES DURING THE COURSE OF THE DEPOSITION. ACCORDINGLY, THE COURT WILL NOT DENY THE MOTION FOR FAILURE TO MEET AND CONFER THIS TIME, BUT EXPECTS THE PARTIES TO ENGAGE IN MEANINGFUL MEET AND CONFER WELL IN ADVANCE OF ANY MOTION DEADLINES IN THE FUTURE (OUTSIDE THE DEPOSITION). TURNING TO THE SUBSTANCE, THE COURT GRANTS IN PART AND DENIES IN PART DEFENDANTS' MOTION AS FOLLOWS: QUESTION 1 IS DENIED. DEFENDANTS HAVE MADE AN INSUFFICIENT SHOWING OF RELEVANCE. QUESTION 2 IS GRANTED. PLAINTIFF DID NOT FULLY ANSWER THE QUESTION. QUESTION 3 IS GRANTED. IT IS FAIR CROSS-EXAMINATION. QUESTION 4 IS DENIED. THE ISSUE IS MORE APPROPRIATE FOR A DOCUMENT REQUEST. QUESTION 5 IS DENIED. PLAINTIFF ANSWERED THE QUESTION. QUESTION 6 IS GRANTED. PLAINTIFF CAN GIVE HER BEST LAY ANSWER, IF SHE HAS ONE. QUESTION 7 IS DENIED ON RELEVANCE AND PRIVACY GROUNDS. (PLEASE SEE SCANNED ORDER FOR DETAILS) JUDGE PRO TEM: GEOFF HOWARD, CLERK: M. GOODMAN, NOT REPORTED. =(302/JPT)

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Petitioner Jane Doe's motion for terminating sanctions and alternative motion for evidentiary and monetary sanctions is denied without prejudice to being re-filed to be heard by the trial judge. Defendant Dr. Timothy Marten's objection based on insufficient notice to hearing the motion on its merits has been waived by his extensive briefing on the merits and submission of factual materials that bear on the merits. Plaintiff's accusation of intentional destruction of relevant documents is a very grave charge and, if true, may warrant some or all of the extraordinary relief sought by this motion. However, based on the paper record on this motion -- most particularly the declaration of Dr. Martens denying the accusation. -- it is not possible for a law and motion judge to determine whether all or any part of the motion should be granted without holding an evidentiary hearing that would likely consume many hours, if not many days. The law and motion department is not able to accommodate such an evidentiary hearing. If one or both parties believe that the motion should be heard before the trial date, they can request that the case be singly assigned to a judge for all purposes. A single assignment motion is made in department 206. Parties to meet and confer as to form of order. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR # 5976 =302/hk

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Renewed Motion For Summary Judgment; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Separate Statement Of Undisputed Material Facts; Declarations And Exhibitsq
Off Calendar Mar-25-2016 Denied
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES: Atty. THOMAS A. MOORE, ESQ. of Brownstone Law Group, PC, Tel No. 650-575-4991 appearing for Petitioner/Plaintiff.
No other appearances made in this matter.

The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Respondent/Defendant Dr. Timothy Marten's renewed motion for summary judgment is denied. Dr. Martens provided sufficient grounds to warrant permitting him to make this renewed motion. Nonetheless, the motion is denied because there are triable issues whether: 1) Mr. Martens committed medical negligence or a medical battery by performing procedures that plaintiff expressly stated that she did not want and 2) plaintiff's claims for medical negligence and medical battery are time-barred. Paragraph 9 of the complaint alleges that plaintiff told Dr. Martens not to penetrate her face and she did not ask for any work around her mouth. Despite that request, Dr. Martens injected fat into plaintiff's face around her mouth and changed the shape of plaintiff's mouth. Plaintiff's declaration substantiates the allegation that Dr. Martin failed to follow her directions. (Doe's Dec. 12:1-12; 13:7-9; 15:9-11; 17:4-20.) Dr. Toth states that plaintiff consented to the mouth work because that was part of the procedures that she consented to. (Toth Dec., 10:3-18.). This conflicting evidence creates a triable issue. Expert testimony is not required. Plaintiff's injury -- Dr. Marten's failure to follow instructions and unilaterally performing a procedure -- is an injury within the common knowledge of laypersons. There are also triable issues regarding the statute of limitations. "[T]he doctrine of equitable tolling requires timely notice, and lack of prejudice, to the defendant, and reasonable and good faith conduct on the part of the plaintiff." (Addison v. State of California (1978) 21 Cal.3d 313, 319). Plaintiff commenced the arbitration within the statute of limitations. There are triable issues regarding lack of prejudice to Dr. Marten and reasonable and good faith conduct by plaintiff. Because the argument was raised for the first time in the reply papers, the court did not consider Dr. Marten's argument based on Belton v. Bowers Ambulance (1999) 20 Cal.4th 928 that there can be no tolling of the 3-year period in CCP 340.5 unless it meets a tolling circumstance expressly listed in the statute. The failure to raise the Belton argument in the moving papers deprived plaintiff of the opportunity to respond to that argument including presenting evidence regarding tolling based on a ground stated in section 340.5. There is also a triable issue whether Dr. Martens is estopped from invoking the statute of limitations. "A defendant will be estopped to invoke the statute of limitations where there has been 'some conduct by the defendant, relied on by the plaintiff, which induces the belated filing of the action.' It is not necessary that the defendant acted in bad faith or intended to mislead the plaintiff. It is sufficient that the defendant's conduct in fact induced the plaintiff to refrain from instituting legal proceedings. '[W]hether an estoppel exists-whether the acts, representations or conduct lulled a party into a sense of security preventing him from instituting proceedings before the running of the statute, and whether the party relied thereon to his prejudice-is a question of fact and not of law.'" (Shaffer v. Debbas (1993) 17 Cal.App.4th 33, 43). There is a triable issue whether Dr. Marten's participation in the arbitration process induced plaintiff into thinking that the parties were in the proper venue. Dr. Marten argues that he did not know about the absence of an arbitration agreement. The trier of fact should resolve what he knew regarding his agreement ( CCP 437c(e)) and whether, under the circumstances of this case, judicial estoppel applies even if he was also ignorant of the arbitration agreement. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: Sean Kane, Not Reported. (302/HK)

Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Pltf, Jane Doe, To Provide Further Answers To Deposition Questions And Produce Documents; Request For Monetary Sanctions
Off Calendar May-19-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No Appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling. DEFENDANT TIMOTHY MARTEN's Motion To Compel Plaintiff, Jane Doe, To Provide Further Answers To Deposition Questions And Produce Documents; Request For Monetary Sanctions: Defendants move for further responses to 11 deposition questions, for production of a document referenced in the deposition, and for monetary sanctions. Plaintiff's deposition has taken place over 5 days so far. The record contains long colloquy between counsel, numerous speaking objections and instructions not to answer on a variety of grounds. Defendants waited until the last day to file this motion without engaging in meet and confer other than what had occurred on the record at the deposition. Plaintiff contends the motion is untimely and that defendants failed adequately to meet and confer. The Court finds that the motion is timely as it was filed within 60 days of defendant receiving the transcript. The Court also finds that although defendants should have engaged in further meet and confer, the parties did meet and confer on the issues during the course of the deposition. Accordingly, the Court will not deny the motion for failure to meet and confer this time, but expects the parties to engage in meaningful meet and confer well in advance of any motion deadlines in the future (outside the deposition). Turning to the substance, the court grants in part and denies in part defendants' motion as follows: Question 1 is denied. Defendants have made an insufficient showing of relevance. Question 2 is granted. Plaintiff did not fully answer the question. Question 3 is granted. It is fair cross-examination. Question 4 is denied. The issue is more appropriate for a document request. Question 5 is denied. Plaintiff answered the question. Question 6 is granted. Plaintiff can give her best lay answer, if she has one. Question 7 is denied on relevance and privacy grounds. Question 8 is denied on relevance grounds and lack of apparent foundation. Question 9 is granted. The question is fair and plaintiff has not provided an answer to it but rather to a different question. Question 10 is denied. The general subject matter is covered elsewhere in the deposition. Motions for sanctions are denied. The plaintiff shall appear for further deposition within 25 days of this order for questioning limited to Questions 2, 3, 6 and 9 and reasonable, directly related, follow-up questions. Total record time shall not exceed 20 minutes. Order is signed in Open Court. Judge Pro Tem: Geoff Howard, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not reported.=(302/JPT)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Jul-22-2013 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: THOMAS MOORE, ESQ., 650.575.4991, FOR PLAINTIFF JANE DOE.
NAFIJA WESTON, ESQ., 925.838.1100, FOR DEFENDANTS TIMOTHY MARTEN AND MARTEN CLINIC OF PLASTIC AND RECONSTRUCTIVE SURGERY.

RULING: Argued and the Court adopted its tentative ruling: DEFENDANTs TIMOTHY MARTEN and MARTEN CLINIC OF PLASTIC AND RECONSTRUCTIVE SURGERY's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT is denied. Defendant Marten ("Defendant") has not met his initial burden of proof to show that there are no triable issues of fact. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 850.) First, Defendant has not presented sufficient evidence to show that the statute of limitations is not subject to equitable estoppel. Defendant has not presented any evidence to controvert the allegations in the Complaint on this issue. Likewise, the separate statement does not address this issue. Second, Plaintiff's evidentiary objections to the declaration of Dr. Toth and the declaration of Nafia Weston are sustained. The supplemental declaration of Nafia Weston, filed in reply, does not cure the evidentiary objection as to the medical records; it still fails to provide a sufficient basis to authenticate these records and to establish that they are business records. See Garibay v. Hemmat, 161 Cal. App. 4th 735 (2008). Even if the Court were to consider the testimony of Dr. Toth, it does not establish that Plaintiff cannot make a prima facie claim for medical negligence, battery and negligent misrepresentation. It does not address the allegations that Plaintiff did not consent to Defendant changing the shape of her mouth, using any procedure that penetrated her face or narrowed her face. At the very least, this creates a triable issue of material fact. (See Declaration of Plaintiff filed in Opposition, 7-8.) (Prevailing party to prepare a form of order.) Judge: Marla J. Miller, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Reporter: Anthony Vaughn #6185.

RESPONDENT TIMOTHY MARTEN'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT is Overruled as to the first and second causes of action and sustained without leave to amend as to the third cause of action. The allegations of equitable tolling and estoppel are sufficient to preclude sustaining the demurrer on statute of limitations grounds. The first and second causes of action are adequately pled.
